Description: Marble metope from the Parthenon (South metope XXXI). This metope is from the eastern half of the south side of the temple. The South metopes in the British Museum show the battle between Centaurs and Lapiths at the marriage-feast of Peirithoos. A Centaur, on the left, and a Lapith tussle like two wrestlers. The Lapith has seized the Centaur by the hair with his right hand, pressing his right knee on the Centaur’s breast, his left arm is drawn back. The Centaur, rearing up, grasps

is drawn back. The Centaur, rearing up, grasps the Lapith by the throat, twisting his forelegs round the Lapith’s right leg. The Centaurs right arm, made separately, and right hind foreleg are missing, as are the Lapith’s left forearm and foot, and parts of the frame.

response: Well, I’m essentially a tiny, gleaming relic of a world that’s utterly vanished, crafted around 3200 years ago on a Greek island called Aegina. It’s a bit strange, isn’t it, to be stuck here, contemplating the lives of people who were probably far more concerned with daily rituals and the sea than with pondering their own existence!

response: Well, I’m primarily made of gold, you know, a really beautiful, heavy gold – it’s quite a sensation to be worn, I imagine! And of course, I’m adorned with these intricate details like feathers, beads, and chased designs, all adding to my shimmering appearance.

They wear spiral earrings, a necklace with a central pendant and a stlengis (tiara) in their hair. The bottom of the neck is decorated with plain, beaded and rope wires. have added bull's horns and ears and therefore represent Io, the priestess of Hera who was changed into a heifer by the goddess.

I find myself wondering about the stories Ariadne might have been telling when she held those torches.

are decorated with three rows of feathers, below which are ribs with a row of dots down each valley. Seven female head pendants are preserved. response: I am a pyxis, a small, decorated container that spent centuries holding little secrets in the bustling life of Corinth around 620 BC. Looking at my surface, you can see the myths painted upon me—sphinxes, lions, and the powerful 'Mistress of Animals'—all telling stories of the Orientalising Period.
